text: The 3D model displays the larger lesion near the right portal pedicle. A wedge biopsy is performed on a small lesion located on the liver capsule.

The surgeon mobilizes the liver by taking down the hepatic oval confluence and removing adhesions between the liver and diaphragm. Intraoperative ultrasound is used to map the intrahepatic vasculature, aiding in the identification of the notch between the right and middle hepatic veins.

The surgeon marks the amylohepatic vein on the liver surface, which is located near the medial aspect of the tumor. Partial right hemoliver mobilization begins by carefully detaching the posterior attachment to the gyrotofascia using a hook artery.
The surgeon approaches the porta hepatis, clips, and divides the cystic duct. The gallbladder is detached from the segment five liver bed in a standard fashion.
The surgeon removes a prominent common bile duct lymph node as part of the lymphadenectomy, ensuring thorough lymphatic examination during the operation.

The surgeon dissects upwards to identify the pole vein branches leading to the right hemoliver. An accessory segment six pole inflow artery is divided with scissors, and the segment six pole vein is isolated using a Maryland robotic instrument.

The surgeon uses a six-inch silk suture to assist in the placement of the Hem-o-lok clip, positioning two clips on the staying side and one on the specimen side before dividing the tissue with robotic scissors. The procedure is performed with precision.

The surgeon dissects and isolates the main right hepatic artery, dividing surrounding lymphatic tissue. An accessory vessel to segment six of the liver is clipped and divided. The right posterior pole vein is visible.

The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) to identify the intrahepatic bile duct, while a bulldog clamp is placed to test the inflow to the right hemoliver. The right hepatic artery is then divided between clips.
The surgeon uses bipolar forceps and a hokari to dissect and isolate the right anterior pole vein. A silk suture is passed around the isolated vein.

The surgeon safely identifies the left pole vein and continues to mobilize the right lobe of the liver by detaching more of its attachments. The lateral aspect of the vena cava is exposed, revealing the short hepatic veins, which are carefully clipped using robotic instruments.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer on the hepatic side to manage larger short hepatic veins. Two clips are placed on these veins before division with robotic scissors. Some clips are reinforced with figure-of-eight sutures.

Traction stitches are placed on both sides of the transection plane. The inflow vessels to the right hemoliver have been ligated and divided, including both the right anterior and right posterior pedicles. The surgeon uses a monopolar cautery device to dissect the first centimeter of the superficial hepatic parenchyma.

The surgical team places a central line for the major hepatic resection. As the procedure progresses deeper into the liver parenchyma, the surgeon switches to an extended vessel sealer to identify and manage crossing branches intrahepatically. The assistant uses laparoscopic suction to clear smoke and irrigate the area with saline.

The surgeon has divided the liver parenchyma down to the right hepatic bile duct, which is now isolated. The robotic stapler is fired across the right hepatic bile duct, transecting it. Concurrently, the right anterior pole vein is being transected.

The liver is opened in a book-like manner as the surgeon continues dissection cephalad toward the notch. The right hepatic vein is stapled flush to the vena cava, forming a neat staple line. The division of hepatic segment eight parenchyma proceeds bloodlessly. The large Makucha ligament is transected using a stapler.

The surgeon divides the remaining coronary and triangular ligaments from the diaphragm, preparing to remove the vascularized specimen. A laparoscopic catch bag is used to extract the specimen through the incision at the jail point. The ICG fluorescence is visible, indicating adequate visualization.
